name: Pre-training
oneliner: Training a model from scratch on massive text via next-token prediction — the most expensive stage, producing a base model with no instruction-following ability.
explanation: Self-supervised: predict x_t from x_1..x_{t-1} on hundreds of billions to trillions of tokens. No human labels needed. Produces a 'base model' that can complete text but doesn't follow instructions. Autoregressive (GPT-style, left-to-right) is dominant for generative models. Chinchilla scaling: N params should train on ~20N tokens, but modern models 'over-train' (smaller model + more tokens = cheaper inference). Cost: LLaMA 2 70B used 1.7M A100 GPU-hours. GPT-4 estimated $50-100M+.
fundamentals: Loss: L = -(1/T)Σ log p_θ(x_t | x_1..x_{t-1}). Standard cross-entropy over vocabulary at each position. Data: raw text, tokenized (BPE/SPM), packed into fixed-length sequences (2K-128K tokens). Hyperparams: lr 1e-4 to 3e-4 (cosine decay), batch 4M-16M tokens/step, AdamW (β1=0.9, β2=0.95, wd=0.1), bf16 mixed precision. Compute: ~6ND FLOPs (N=params, D=tokens). 70B on 2T tokens ≈ 8.4×10²³ FLOPs.

foundational_papers.title: Attention Is All You Need
Language Models are Few-Shot Learners (GPT-3)
Training Compute-Optimal Large Language Models (Chinchilla)
LLaMA: Open and Efficient Foundation Language Models

name: CPT
oneliner: Additional pre-training on domain-specific data (code, medical, legal) to inject specialized knowledge — same loss as pre-training, 2-10% of the cost.
explanation: Takes a general base model and runs more pre-training on domain corpus. Same next-token prediction objective but shifted data distribution. Key challenge: catastrophic forgetting — mitigated by mixing 10-30% general data, lower lr (2-10× lower). Examples: Code Llama (500B code tokens), Meditron (medical), SaulLM (legal), Minerva (math/science). Cost: 2-10% of original pre-training.
fundamentals: Loss: identical to pre-training (autoregressive CE). Only data distribution changes. Hyperparams: lr 1e-5 to 5e-5, cosine or constant schedule. Data mixing: ~70-90% domain + 10-30% general replay.

foundational_papers.title: Don't Stop Pretraining: Adapt Pretrained Language Models to Diverse Text Domains
Code Llama: Open Foundation Models for Code
Minerva: Solving Quantitative Reasoning Problems with Language Models

name: SFT
oneliner: Training on (instruction, response) pairs — loss computed only on response tokens, not the prompt. Quality >> quantity (LIMA: 1K examples can suffice).
explanation: Transforms base model into instruction-following assistant. Loss masking on prompts (CE only on response tokens). Dataset sizes: 10K-1M. Key datasets: FLAN (1800+ tasks), OpenAssistant (161K messages), Alpaca (52K GPT-3.5-generated), ShareGPT, UltraChat (1.5M). Cost: ~0.1-1% of pre-training. Sits after pre-training/CPT, before preference alignment.
fundamentals: Loss: L = -(1/|y|)Σ log p_θ(y_t | x, y_1..y_{t-1}). Prompt x tokens masked. Data: {messages: [{role:user, content:...}, {role:assistant, content:...}]}. Hyperparams: lr 1e-5 to 2e-5, 1-5 epochs. Often uses LoRA/QLoRA.

foundational_papers.title: Training language models to follow instructions with human feedback (InstructGPT)
Scaling Instruction-Finetuned Language Models (FLAN v2)
LIMA: Less Is More for Alignment
Self-Instruct: Aligning Language Models with Self-Generated Instructions

name: RLHF
oneliner: Train reward model from preferences, optimize LLM via PPO + KL penalty. Complex (4 models in memory) but the original alignment breakthrough.
explanation: Three stages: (1) SFT, (2) reward model on preference pairs (Bradley-Terry), (3) PPO against reward with KL penalty. 4 models simultaneously: policy, reference, reward, value. Used by InstructGPT, ChatGPT, Claude (with Constitutional AI), Llama 2-Chat, Gemini. Cost: 5-20% of pre-training. Complex and unstable — motivated DPO/ORPO/KTO.
fundamentals: RM loss: L = -E[log σ(r(x,y_w) - r(x,y_l))]. PPO: max E[r(x,y) - β·KL(π_θ||π_ref)]. PPO clips: L = -min(ratio·Â, clip(ratio,1±ε)·Â). β 0.01-0.2, ε=0.2. Preference data: 50K-500K comparisons.

foundational_papers.title: Training language models to follow instructions with human feedback (InstructGPT)
Deep reinforcement learning from human preferences
Proximal Policy Optimization Algorithms
Learning to summarize from human feedback
Constitutional AI: Harmlessness from AI Feedback
Llama 2: Open Foundation and Fine-Tuned Chat Models

name: DPO
oneliner: Reparameterizes RLHF into a classification loss on preference pairs — no reward model, no PPO, 2 models in memory. The default open-source alignment method.
explanation: Optimal RLHF policy satisfies r(x,y) = β·log[π_θ/π_ref] + Z(x). Substituting into Bradley-Terry cancels reward model — reduces to supervised loss. 2-5× cheaper than RLHF. Failure modes: distribution shift, overfitting to chosen, reference sensitivity. Default for open-source: Zephyr, Tulu 2, Llama 3.1, many community models.
fundamentals: Loss: L = -E[log σ(β·log(π_θ(y_w|x)/π_ref(y_w|x)) - β·log(π_θ(y_l|x)/π_ref(y_l|x)))]. β 0.1-0.5. lr 1e-6 to 5e-7. Same preference data as RLHF RM.

foundational_papers.title: Direct Preference Optimization: Your Language Model is Secretly a Reward Model
Zephyr: Direct Distillation of LM Alignment
A General Theoretical Paradigm to Understand Learning from Human Feedback (IPO)

name: ORPO
oneliner: Combines SFT + preference alignment in one stage via odds-ratio penalty — no separate SFT, no reference model. Single-stage, single-model.
explanation: SFT+DPO first increases prob of both chosen AND rejected (SFT), then separates (DPO) — inefficient. ORPO adds odds-ratio penalty to SFT loss directly. Single stage on preference data applied to base model. No reference model. ~50% cheaper than SFT+DPO.
fundamentals: L = L_SFT + λ·L_OR. L_OR = -log σ(log(odds(y_w|x)/odds(y_l|x))). odds(y|x) = p(y|x)/(1-p(y|x)). λ 0.1-1.0. No reference model.

foundational_papers.title: ORPO: Monolithic Preference Optimization without Reference Model

name: KTO
oneliner: Only needs thumbs-up/down per response (not paired preferences) — grounded in prospect theory's loss aversion.
explanation: DPO/RLHF need PAIRED preferences. KTO works with UNPAIRED binary labels (prompt, response, good/bad). Based on prospect theory: losses loom larger than gains (~2:1). Desirable responses rewarded; undesirable penalized more heavily. Reference point = expected KL divergence. Requires reference model.
fundamentals: Desirable: v = σ(β·log(π_θ/π_ref) - z_ref). Undesirable: v = σ(z_ref - β·log(π_θ/π_ref)). Loss: -E_w[λ_w·v(y_w)] - E_l[λ_l·v(y_l)], λ_l > λ_w. Data: {prompt, response, label: bool}.

foundational_papers.title: KTO: Model Alignment as Prospect Theoretic Optimization
Prospect Theory: An Analysis of Decision under Risk

name: SimPO
oneliner: Reference-free DPO: length-normalized log-prob as implicit reward + target margin γ. Halves memory vs DPO.
explanation: Removes reference model entirely. Implicit reward = average log-prob per token (length-normalized). Adds margin γ: chosen must exceed rejected by at least γ. No reference = halves memory. β 2.0-2.5 (higher than DPO). Competitive or better than DPO on AlpacaEval 2 / MT-Bench.
fundamentals: L = -E[log σ(β·avg_logp(y_w|x) - β·avg_logp(y_l|x) - γ)]. avg_logp = (1/|y|)Σ log π_θ(y_t|x,y_<t). No π_ref. β 2.0-2.5, γ 0.5-1.5.

foundational_papers.title: SimPO: Simple Preference Optimization with a Reference-Free Reward

name: LoRA
expansion: Low-Rank Adaptation of Large Language Models
oneliner: Freeze base weights, add trainable low-rank bypass W₀ + (α/r)·B·A. Typically 0.1-0.6% of params. Zero inference overhead after merging.
explanation: Fine-tuning updates have low intrinsic rank. Freeze W₀, add ΔW = BA where B∈ℝ^(d×r), A∈ℝ^(r×d), r<<d. B init to 0 (starts from pretrained). Scaling α/r. Apply to Q,K,V,O + FFN for best results. r typically 8-16. Merge: W = W₀ + (α/r)·BA. The default PEFT method.
fundamentals: Forward: h = W₀x + (α/r)·B·A·x. LLaMA 7B r=16 all linear: ~40M params (0.6%). Memory: ~14.8GB (vs ~84GB full FT). Merge: one-time matrix add. Files: adapter_model.safetensors (~80MB) + adapter_config.json.

foundational_papers.title: LoRA: Low-Rank Adaptation of Large Language Models
Intrinsic Dimensionality Explains the Effectiveness of Language Model Fine-Tuning
LoRA+: Efficient Low Rank Adaptation of Large Models

name: QLoRA
oneliner: Base model in 4-bit NF4 + LoRA in fp16. Fine-tune 65B on a single 48GB GPU. Three innovations: NF4, double quantization, paged optimizers.
explanation: Frozen base weights quantized to NF4 (4-bit). LoRA adapters in bf16 compensate for quant error. NF4 optimal for Gaussian weights. Double quant: quantize the quantization constants. Paged optimizers: CPU↔GPU memory paging. 65B: ~130GB fp16 → ~41GB NF4. Quality matches 16-bit full FT.
fundamentals: Forward: h = Dequant_NF4(W₀_q)·x + (α/r)·B·A·x. Memory 65B: ~33.5GB base + ~1.6GB adapters/optimizer + ~4-8GB activations ≈ 40-44GB. NF4: 16 levels at N(0,1) quantile positions. Double quant: block-64 absmax → quantize to 8-bit in blocks of 256.

foundational_papers.title: QLoRA: Efficient Finetuning of Quantized Language Models
The case for 4-bit precision: k-bit Inference Scaling Laws

name: DoRA
oneliner: Decompose weights into magnitude + direction, apply LoRA to direction only. More like full FT behavior, negligible extra params.
explanation: LoRA couples magnitude and direction changes; full FT changes direction more. DoRA: W₀ = m·(V/||V||), freeze V, add LoRA to V, train m separately. Forward: W' = m·(V+(α/r)·BA)/||V+(α/r)·BA||. Extra: just m ∈ ℝ^(d_out) per layer. Consistently outperforms LoRA. PEFT: use_dora=True.
fundamentals: W' = m·(V+(α/r)·BA)/||V+(α/r)·BA||_c. Extra params: d_out per layer. LLaMA 7B: 0.004% overhead. Based on weight normalization.

foundational_papers.title: DoRA: Weight-Decomposed Low-Rank Adaptation
Weight Normalization: A Simple Reparameterization to Accelerate Training

name: IA3
expansion: Infused Adapter by Inhibiting and Amplifying Inner Activations
oneliner: Learns rescaling vectors (not matrices) for K, V, FFN activations — 10-100× fewer params than LoRA. Best for few-shot and massive multi-task serving.
explanation: Element-wise scaling: l_k for keys, l_v for values, l_ff for FFN intermediate. K'=l_k⊙K, V'=l_v⊙V, FFN'=l_ff⊙FFN_mid. LLaMA 7B: 614K params (0.009%). Initialized to ones. Quality ceiling lower than LoRA for large FT, but excellent for few-shot and serving thousands of adapters.
fundamentals: K' = l_k ⊙ (W_K·x). V' = l_v ⊙ (W_V·x). FFN: h = W_down·(l_ff ⊙ act(W_up·x)). Params: d_k+d_v+d_ff per layer. LLaMA 7B: 19,200/layer × 32 = 614K. Optimizer: ~5MB.

foundational_papers.title: Few-Shot Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning is Better and Cheaper than In-Context Learning

name: PEFT
oneliner: Umbrella term for all methods adapting LLMs by training a tiny fraction of params — AND the HuggingFace library (LoRA, QLoRA, DoRA, IA3, prefix tuning, etc.).
explanation: Concept: any technique updating far fewer than all parameters. Families: additive (LoRA, adapters), soft prompt (prefix/prompt tuning), selective (BitFit), reparameterization (LoRA). Library: get_peft_model(base, config), saves adapter files (MBs not GBs). Supports merging, multi-adapter, quantized bases. De facto standard.
fundamentals: API: LoraConfig(r=16, lora_alpha=32, target_modules=[...]). Save: adapter_model.safetensors + adapter_config.json. Load: PeftModel.from_pretrained(). Merge: model.merge_and_unload(). Methods: LoRA, QLoRA, DoRA, AdaLoRA, LoRA+, rsLoRA, IA3, prefix tuning, prompt tuning, OFT, BOFT.

foundational_papers.title: Scaling Down to Scale Up: A Guide to Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning
Parameter-Efficient Transfer Learning for NLP
LoRA: Low-Rank Adaptation of Large Language Models

name: Full Fine-Tuning
oneliner: Update every parameter — ~12N bytes memory, maximum expressiveness, risks catastrophic forgetting, produces full model copies.
explanation: Train all params. Memory: ~12N bytes (weights + gradients + Adam states + master weights). LLaMA 7B: ~84GB. Requires DeepSpeed ZeRO or FSDP. Preferred for large datasets, domain shift, max quality. Risks: catastrophic forgetting. All -instruct/-chat from major labs use full SFT+RLHF.
fundamentals: Memory: 7B→84GB, 13B→156GB, 70B→840GB. ZeRO-1: shard optimizer. ZeRO-2: +gradients. ZeRO-3: +parameters. lr 1e-5 to 5e-5, 1-3 epochs.

foundational_papers.title: Training language models to follow instructions with human feedback (InstructGPT)
ZeRO: Memory Optimizations Toward Training Trillion Parameter Models

name: Adapters (General Concept)
expansion: Adapter Modules — the broad category of injected trainable modules
oneliner: Small trainable modules inserted into frozen models. Houlsby (serial bottleneck), Pfeiffer (efficient), parallel, LoRA are all adapter variants.
explanation: Houlsby (2019): bottleneck d→r→d + residual, after each sub-layer. Pfeiffer: one per layer (post-FFN). Parallel (He 2022): alongside sub-layer. LoRA: parallel without nonlinearity, zero-init B. Tags: -adapter (bottleneck), -lora, -merged (folded in), -unmerged (separate).
fundamentals: Houlsby: adapter(x) = x + W_up·σ(W_down·x). 4dr params/layer. Pfeiffer: 2dr/layer. LoRA: 2dr per target module. Inference: Houlsby 5-10% latency. LoRA merged: zero.

foundational_papers.title: Parameter-Efficient Transfer Learning for NLP
Towards a Unified View of Parameter-Efficient Transfer Learning
AdapterFusion: Non-Destructive Task Composition for Transfer Learning
